home.heroSubtitle: From the banks of the Sanaga to the southern forests: discover the history, languages and traditions of the Beti, Bulu, Ewondo and Fang peoples.
home.heroAlt: Equatorial rainforest of southern Cameroon at sunrise
home.historyCardText: From the great Bantu migrations to the crossing of the Sanaga, follow our ancestors' path to present-day Cameroon.
home.historyCardAlt: Landscape of hills and forests in the Centre Region
home.languagesCardText: Ewondo, Bulu, Fang, Eton… Discover the Beti languages and start learning them with our interactive lessons.
home.languagesCardAlt: Two people in conversation in a village
home.traditionsCardText: The Mvett, bikutsi, rites of passage: dive into the richness of Beti arts and spirituality.
home.featuredStoryTitle: Kulu the Tortoise and the Sky Drum
home.featuredStoryExcerpt: In the forest, Kulu the tortoise — small but cunning — hears of a magic drum that Zamba keeps in the sky. How will she make it sound all the way down to the village of men?
home.featuredAlt: Path through the equatorial forest at dusk

newsletter.title: Stay close to the village
newsletter.text: Each month, receive a folktale, a word to learn and the latest additions to the site.
newsletter.success: Thank you! Your subscription has been recorded.

history.title: History of the Beti-Fang peoples
history.intro: The Beti, Bulu, Ewondo, Fang and related groups form the Beti-Pahuin family — Bantu-speaking peoples settled in Cameroon's Centre and South regions, as well as in Gabon and Equatorial Guinea.
history.e1Text: The ancestors of the Beti-Fang belong to the vast movement of Bantu-speaking peoples who, over several millennia, spread from the present-day Nigeria–Cameroon borderlands across Central and Southern Africa, carrying agriculture and iron-working with them.
history.e1Alt: Stylized map of the Bantu migrations across Central Africa
history.e2Text: Oral tradition recounts that the Beti, pressed by conflicts from the north, crossed the Sanaga River to reach the southern forests. Legend says they crossed on the back of a giant serpent, Ngan Medza — a founding narrative passed down through the generations.
history.e2Alt: The Sanaga River bordered by dense forest
history.e3Text: Beti society was organized around patrilineal lineages (mvog) rather than centralized kingdoms. Authority rested with family heads and notables, surrounded by the council of elders. Prestige was measured by wisdom, hospitality and the ability to bring people together.
history.e3Alt: Traditional house and family courtyard in a Beti village
history.e4Text: Kamerun became a German protectorate in 1884. The Beti and Bulu lands endured forced plantation labour and railway construction, but also resisted — notably the Bulu war (1899-1901). Charles Atangana became paramount chief of the Ewondo and Bene under the colonial administration.
history.e5Text: After the First World War, Cameroon was divided between France and Britain under League of Nations mandates. Beti country, in the French zone, saw the spread of cocoa farming, schools and Christian missions — a profound social transformation.
history.e5Alt: Cocoa plantation in the Centre Region
history.e6Text: Martin-Paul Samba, a Bulu officer turned resister, executed in 1914; Charles Atangana, Ewondo paramount chief, builder and diplomat; and later renowned writers such as Mongo Beti and Ferdinand Oyono, who carried the voice of Beti country into world literature.
history.e6Alt: Portrait of a Cameroonian notable from the early 20th century
history.e7Text: Cameroon gained independence on 1 January 1960. Yaoundé, in the heart of Ewondo country, became the capital. Beti cultures now shine through bikutsi, literature, academia and an active diaspora working to pass on language and traditions.
history.e7Alt: Panoramic view of Yaoundé, capital of Cameroon
history.clansIntro: Among the Beti, everyone belongs to a patrilineal lineage, the mvog ("house of…"), tracing back to a founding ancestor. To know your mvog is to know your alliances, your marriage prohibitions and your place in the community. The tree below presents the main Beti-Pahuin groupings in simplified form.
history.clansNote: Simplified representation for educational purposes: real genealogies are far richer and vary between family traditions.
history.treeBetiText: Ewondo, Eton, Bene, northern Bulu… settled around Yaoundé and in the Lékié.
history.treeBuluText: Around Ebolowa and Sangmélima, in the South Region.
history.treeFangText: From southern Cameroon into Gabon and Equatorial Guinea.
history.treeOthersText: Mvele, Bebele, Mengisa, Batsenga and other communities of the Sanaga valley.

languages.title: The Beti languages
languages.intro: The Beti languages belong to the Bantu A70 group. Largely mutually intelligible, they form a continuum in which every community recognizes its own voice. Ewondo has historically served as a lingua franca around Yaoundé.
languages.ewondoWriting: Latin alphabet (General Alphabet of Cameroon Languages)
languages.buluWriting: Latin alphabet; rich tradition of missionary literature
languages.fangSpeakers: ≈ 1,000,000 (Cameroon, Gabon, Equatorial Guinea — to be verified)
languages.fangRegions: Ntem valley and across the southern borders
languages.mapCaption: Schematic map of Cameroon: each colour shows the approximate zone where the language is most spoken. Real linguistic boundaries are fluid.

learn.title: Learn Ewondo
learn.intro: Short, practical lessons, starting with Ewondo, the most widely spoken Beti language. Every phrase comes with pronunciation, French and English translations — and soon audio recorded by native speakers.
learn.quizIntro: Eight random questions: match each phrase to its translation.

traditions.title: Traditions & living arts
traditions.intro: Music, dance, epics, spirituality and rites of passage: the threads that weave Beti identity and still inspire contemporary Cameroon.
traditions.musicTitle: Music: from the mvet to bikutsi
traditions.musicMvett: The mvet is both an instrument — a harp-zither made from a raffia stem, calabash resonators and strings — and an entire poetic art. The mvet player accompanies epic narratives that can last whole nights.
traditions.musicBikutsi: Bikutsi, a 6/8 rhythm born from Ewondo women's songs, went electric in the 1970s-80s to become one of Cameroon's great popular genres, carried by artists such as Anne-Marie Nzié and Les Têtes Brûlées.
traditions.musicChoral: Choral singing, women's polyphonies and orchestras of drums and xylophones (mendzaŋ) still set the rhythm of feasts, mourning and religious celebrations.
traditions.musicAlt: Musician playing a traditional stringed instrument
traditions.danceIntro: Every great occasion has its dances: the essani, a dance of honour performed at the funerals of notables; the ozila, a dance of rejoicing; and the lively choreography of bikutsi, feet striking the ground — bi-kut-si literally means "let us beat the earth".
traditions.danceText2: Dance is a language: it honours the dead, welcomes guests, accompanies initiations and tells with the body what words leave unsaid.
traditions.danceAlt: Dancers in traditional dress at a village festival
traditions.epicsTitle: The Mvett, epic of the Ekang
traditions.epicsP1: The Mvett epic — shared by the Fang, Bulu and Beti — sings the endless struggle between the mortals of Okü and the immortals of Engong, in quest of the secret of immortality. It is a monument of Central African oral literature.
traditions.epicsP2: The mbômô-mvet, the initiated bard, is at once musician, poet, historian and philosopher. His training takes years under a master. Today, writers and scholars are working to transcribe and preserve this heritage.
traditions.epicsAlt: Storyteller by a fire at night, surrounded by his audience
traditions.beliefsP1: In the traditional Beti worldview, Zamba (Nzambe among the Bulu, Nzame among the Fang) is the supreme being, creator of the world. The ancestors remain present among the living: they are honoured and consulted, and their blessing accompanies every great decision.
traditions.beliefsP2: The nganga — diviner-healer — heals with plants, interprets signs and restores harmony between the visible and the invisible. Today, Christianity and tradition coexist and intertwine in the spiritual life of most families.
traditions.beliefsAlt: Sacred forest bathed in early-morning mist
traditions.ritesBirth: Birth: the child is welcomed with protective rites and receives a meaningful name, often that of an ancestor or elder (the mbombó, the namesake), creating a special bond between them.
traditions.ritesInitiation: Initiation: in the past, the Sso — the great initiation of young Ewondo men, marked by trials of endurance — opened the way to adult status. Though no longer practised, this rite remains a major landmark of cultural memory.
traditions.ritesMarriage: Marriage: bridewealth (the "dot") seals the alliance between two families — not a "purchase", but an exchange of respect and commitments celebrated with warm ceremonies where both lineages meet.
traditions.ritesFuneral: Funerals: mourning is a powerful communal moment. Wakes, songs, dances of honour such as the essani and, later, end-of-mourning celebrations accompany the deceased to the world of the ancestors.

games.title: Traditional games & sports
games.intro: Strategy games, wrestling, riddles: play is a school of life — teaching arithmetic, courage, eloquence and respect for one's opponent.
games.wrestlingDesc: A sport of agility and strength practised between villages during festival seasons, wrestling pits two opponents who each try to bring the other to the ground, under the gaze of the elders and to the beat of the drums.
games.wrestlingHow: Wrestlers face off in a circle formed by the crowd. Any hold above the belt is allowed; the first whose back or shoulders touch the ground loses. Bouts are short, refereed by an elder, and end with an embrace.
games.wrestlingNote: Wrestling forges courage and humility: victory honours the village, but arrogance dishonours the victor.
games.wrestlingAlt: Two wrestlers competing before a village crowd
games.songoDesc: Songo is the Beti variant of Africa's great sowing game (the mancala family, like awalé). It is played on a wooden board carved with two rows of seven holes, using seeds or pebbles.
games.songoHow: Each player sows their seeds one by one into the holes, following the direction of play. Depending on where the last seed lands, opposing seeds are captured. The goal: gather more than your opponent. Matches between masters draw spectators and last for hours.
games.songoNote: A game of mental arithmetic and patience, songo is traditionally played by elders and notables — it is said to teach a chief's foresight.
games.songoAlt: Wooden songo board with seeds, set on the ground
games.abbiaDesc: Abbia was a game of chance played with chips carved from fruit stones, each finely engraved with motifs — animals, scenes of life, symbols. These chips rank today among the finest miniatures of Cameroonian art.
games.abbiaHow: Players placed their stakes and cast the chips; the combination of engraved faces decided the winnings. Banned in colonial times, the game has all but disappeared, but its chips are kept in museums around the world.
games.abbiaNote: Beyond the game itself, abbia chips form a veritable engraved encyclopedia of old Beti life.
games.abbiaAlt: Abbia chips engraved with animal motifs
games.childrenDesc: Skipping rope, stilts, hide-and-seek among the coffee bushes, little bark canoes on the streams… and speed games such as ndamba (a ball of woven vines) foreshadowing Cameroon's passion for football.
games.childrenHow: Most games are played by moonlight after the evening meal: singing circles, riddle contests, animal imitations. Elders watch and correct — play is also a lesson.
games.childrenNote: In the village at night, to play is to learn: the language, the songs, the rules of life together.
games.childrenAlt: Children playing a circle game at sunset
games.riddlesDesc: Storytelling evenings often open with riddle contests: an elder throws out the enigma and the audience answers in chorus. It is the school of quick wit and fine speech.
games.riddle1Q: My house has only one central pillar. Who am I?
games.riddle2Q: I cross the river without ever getting wet. Who am I?
games.riddle3Q: Cut off my head and I do not die; I always grow back. Who am I?
games.riddlesNote: Riddles sharpen observation of nature and prepare children for the art of proverbs.
games.riddlesAlt: Village evening gathering around the fire

stories.title: Folktales & oral tradition
stories.intro: The voice of the evening gatherings: folktales, proverbs and epic excerpts, transcribed in French and English. Audio versions told by storytellers are coming soon.
stories.s1Title: Kulu the Tortoise and the Sky Drum
stories.s1Excerpt: Small but cunning, Kulu the tortoise wants to sound the drum that Zamba keeps in the sky.
stories.s2Excerpt: A tale about pride: Zé the panther, too proud to share, learns the price of solitude the hard way.
stories.s3Excerpt: A hunter shares his last catch with a stranger — and receives far more than he gave.
stories.s4Title: At the Gates of Engong (Mvett-inspired excerpt)
stories.s4Excerpt: A young mortal of Okü dreams of stealing the secret of immortality from the land of Engong.
stories.contributeText: Do you know a folktale, a proverb, a riddle? Entrust it to us: we will transcribe and publish it in your name, with your family's consent.
stories.formStoryText: Your story (in the language of your choice)
stories.formSuccess: Thank you! We will contact you very soon.

about.title: About Tribu Beti
about.intro: A community project to preserve, pass on and celebrate the heritage of the Beti, Bulu, Ewondo, Fang and related peoples.
about.missionP1: To gather in one accessible place the memory of the Beti-Fang peoples: history, languages, music, folktales, games and know-how. To offer youth and the diaspora a bridge back to the village, and the world a respectful gateway into our cultures.
about.missionP2: All cultural content is published under a free license (CC BY-SA 4.0) so that schools, associations and families can share and enrich it.
about.whyP1: Every year, elders pass away taking with them folktales, songs and words that no one wrote down. The Beti languages remain alive, but transmission is weakening in the cities and in the diaspora.
about.whyP2: Digitizing does not replace the evening gathering: it reaches a hand between generations, so that a child born in Paris, Douala or Montreal can hear the voice of their grandparents.
about.peoplesIntro: The Beti-Pahuin family numbers over three million people across Cameroon, Gabon, Equatorial Guinea and São Tomé, speaking closely related languages of the Bantu A70 group.
about.peoplesBeti: Beti: Ewondo, Eton, Bene and neighbouring groups, around Yaoundé and the Lékié — keepers of bikutsi and a rich literary tradition.
about.peoplesBulu: Bulu: in the South Region, around Ebolowa and Sangmélima; their language was among the first in the area to be widely written.
about.peoplesFang: Fang: from Cameroon's Ntem valley into Gabon and Equatorial Guinea — masters of the mvett and of world-renowned statuary.
about.peoplesOthers: Related groups: Mvele, Bebele, Mengisa, Batsenga and other communities of the Sanaga valley, some of whose languages are threatened.
about.visionP1: Coming next: audio recordings by native speakers for every lesson, folktales read aloud by storytellers, an interactive map of the mvog, teaching kits for schools, and a community contribution space moderated by elders and linguists.
about.creditsText: Our thanks to the families, storytellers and teachers who feed this site, and to the researchers whose work on Beti languages and cultures guided our texts. Any errors are our own: report them and they will be corrected with gratitude.

contact.title: Contact
contact.intro: A question, a correction, a story to share? Write to us — every message is read.
contact.errEmail: Please enter a valid email address.
contact.success: Thank you! Your message has been sent. We will reply shortly.
contact.contributeText: Family or ceremony photos (with the consent of those pictured), folktales and proverbs, language corrections, audio recordings: every contribution is precious. Tell us in your message what you would like to share, and we will guide you step by step.

contact.about.founderBio: Coco Nkomba built tribubeti.org as an act of love and of historical urgency — because he understands, as all diaspora community members eventually understand, that distance is time's co-conspirator. Families scatter. Grandparents die. Languages thin to a few remembered phrases. The day arrives when a grandchild can no longer ask an elder what a proverb means.
contact.about.contributorsText: This project exists because of community members who share stories, songs, genealogies, and memories. Your voice belongs here.

contact.contribute.title: Contribute to the Project
contact.contribute.intro: Your voice belongs here. Every story, every song, every lineage name is part of preserving our collective heritage.
contact.contribute.whyP1: tribubeti.org is not a closed project. It is an open house — a space where the memory of our peoples gathers, intersects, and is passed on.
contact.contribute.whyP2: If you grew up listening to a story, if you know a song that few still remember, if you carry the name of your lineage — then this project needs you too.
contact.contribute.methodsIntro: All forms of memory are welcome. Here is how you can participate:
contact.contribute.methodStoriesDesc: Origin myths, folktales, historical accounts, life testimonies. In French, Beti, Bulu, Ewondo, or Fang.
contact.contribute.methodSongsDesc: Traditional songs, lullabies, mourning chants, war songs. Send an audio or video link, or written lyrics.
contact.contribute.methodLineageDesc: Family trees, clan names, lineages, migrations. Help us map families and their journeys across time.
contact.contribute.methodPhotosDesc: Old or recent photographs, letters, records, postcards. Each image is a fragment of memory.
contact.contribute.methodProverbsDesc: Proverbs, sayings, riddles, idiomatic expressions with their meaning and context of use.
contact.contribute.methodCorrectionsDesc: Spotted an error? An approximation? Write to us. Rigor is a form of respect.
contact.contribute.howIntro: The process is simple and open to everyone.
contact.contribute.step1Desc: Gather your texts, audio or video recordings, photos, or documents. Any format is accepted.
contact.contribute.step2Desc: Use the form below. Describe what you're sharing, attach your files, and indicate whether you'd like to be credited.
contact.contribute.step3Desc: Your contribution is reviewed, then integrated into the site in the appropriate language. We'll contact you if we have questions.
contact.contribute.step4Desc: Once published, your contribution appears on the site with your name (if you wish) in the contributors section.
contact.contribute.formIntro: Fill out the form below. All fields marked with an asterisk (*) are required.
contact.contribute.contactFallback: Prefer to write directly? Reach us at:
contact.contribute.guidelinesP1: Respect. Contributions must honor the people, families, and traditions referenced. No discriminatory content will be accepted.
contact.contribute.guidelinesP2: Authenticity. Indicate your sources: an elder consulted, a reference book, a lived experience. Traceability strengthens the project's credibility.
contact.contribute.guidelinesP3: Credit. Every contributor is acknowledged, unless anonymity is requested. You retain ownership of your contribution; tribubeti.org shares it in the spirit of collective preservation.
